data.title: What's next for investors after the AI rally supercharged stocks in May?
data.description:
By Philip van Doorn

Also in Weekend Reads: A way to invest in SpaceX before its IPO and advice from the Moneyist

Most sectors of the S&P 500 were down during May. But tech stocks soared again.

Through Friday at 11:45 a.m. ET, the S&P 500 was up 5.3% in May, driven by a remarkable rally in the information-technology sector. Here is a breakdown of how the 11 sectors of the S&P 500 have performed, with the full index at the bottom: 

   Sector or index           May price change  2026 price change  2025 price change 
   Information technology               16.0%              23.7%              23.3% 
   Consumer discretionary                3.0%               4.3%               5.3% 
   Healthcare                            2.2%              -3.8%              12.5% 
   Materials                            -0.2%              11.9%               8.4% 
   Communication services               -0.5%               9.4%              32.4% 
   Industrials                          -0.7%              11.7%              17.7% 
   Financial                            -1.0%              -5.9%              13.3% 
   Real estate                          -1.1%               9.5%              -0.3% 
   Consumer staples                     -3.4%               6.4%               1.3% 
   Utilities                            -5.6%               3.6%              12.7% 
   Energy                               -6.5%              23.8%               5.0% 
   S&P 500 index                         5.3%              10.9%              16.4% 
                                                                       Source: LSEG

All price changes in this article exclude dividends.

Within tech, investors and traders have continued to focus on semiconductor manufacturers. Micron (MU) was up 88% for May, exceeded among the S&P 500 SPX only by Dell (DELL), which had doubled for the month. Dell was up 29% Friday morning following a very strong earnings report.

The iShares Semiconductor ETF SOXX was up 24% for the month for a 2026 gain of 89%, following a gain of 40% during 2025.

Reaction:

-- Micron investors are partying like it's 1987. Analysts say the stock still looks pretty cheap.

-- The surge in chip stocks this year is putting the dot-com rally to shame

-- This stock-market strategy is a smart way to play Micron and the memory-chip rally

But the tech rally was expanding beyond chip makers. The S&P 500 Software and Services Index was up14.8% for May, led by Datadog (DDOG), which was up 70% for the month. Through April, the S&P 500 Software and Services Index had fallen 19.1% in 2026 amid concerns that generative artificial intelligence would be a rising threat to mature systems developers. Microsoft (MSFT) - the third largest stock in the S&P 500 by market capitalization - followed this pattern with a 9.1% gain for May, following a 15.7% decline from the end of 2025 through April.

Software industry coverage:

-- Snowflake's stock blasts off, fueled by AI acceleration and deeper Amazon partnership

-- Okta shares rise on earnings beat and AI-agent opportunity

-- Is Microsoft ditching OpenAI for Anthropic?

-- Anthropic nears $1 trillion valuation, leapfrogging OpenAI

More: These S&P 500 stocks have soared the most during the AI-driven May rally

Looking past this remarkable run-up

One fascinating aspect of this year's stock-market rally has been that it has been driven by earnings - or by expectations for rising profits. We can see this by comparing forward price/earnings ratios for the S&P 500 and its sectors to those at the end of 2025. These are prices divided by consensus earnings-per-share estimates for rolling 12-month periods among analysts polled by LSEG, weighted by market cap. This time the sectors are sorted alphabetically, with the full index at the bottom: 

   Sector or index           Forward P/E  Forward P/E as of Dec. 31 
   Communication services           21.2                       22.2 
   Consumer discretionary           26.7                       29.8 
   Consumer staples                 22.7                       20.9 
   Energy                           14.3                       15.6 
   Financial                        14.5                       16.7 
   Healthcare                       17.0                       18.4 
   Industrials                      24.7                       24.2 
   Information technology           23.9                       26.8 
   Materials                        17.2                       19.3 
   Real estate                      36.9                       35.2 
   Utilities                        17.8                       17.9 
   S&P 500 index                    20.9                       22.5 
                                                       Source: LSEG

The S&P 500's forward P/E has declined to 20.9 from 22.5 this year. The forward P/E ratios have declined for eight of the 11 sectors, as 10 of the sectors have shown year-to-date gains.

When a company reports increasing revenue and earnings, analysts from brokerage and research firms will respond by raising their estimates for the coming quarters. This action can support rising share prices over the long term.

Ed Clissold, the chief U.S. Strategist at Ned Davis Research, pointed out in a note to clients on Wednesday that consensus estimates were calling for a 31.8% increase in profit for the S&P 500 in 2026. "Growth rates this high are normally reserved for the early days of economic expansions when earnings are rebounding from depressed levels, not in year seven of an expansion," he wrote.

Here are five looks at what the market might have in store from here:

-- The AI capital-spending boom looks historic. Eleven other booms suggest a bust is inevitable.

-- Why double-digit earnings growth won't stop the next bear market

-- Stocks just scored a trifecta of record closes for the first time in 2026. What to expect next.

-- Stocks are riding an earnings hot streak - but investors are facing a summer that's rife with risks

-- How a stock market 'melt-up' could carry the S&P 500 to 8,000 or beyond

A bull-market alternative to semiconductors and tech

Through May 28, the Dow Jones Transportation Average had more than doubled the total return of the S&P 500.

Tomi Kilgore looked into technical factors driving a rally for transportation stocks and explained how transportation companies benefit from the AI build-out.

Bond yields rise as bond prices fall - action that threatens the stock market

How high do long-term interest rates have to get before enough investors are tempted by bond yields to push the stock market down?

Joy Wiltermuth talked with money managers about the drivers of bond-market volatility, including inflation, and how expectations for the Federal Reserve's interest-rate policies might factor into investors' money moves.

Two other moves investors are making:

-- Investors are rushing to buy TIPS ETFs to beat inflation. They could end up losing.

-- Annuity payouts are the highest they've been in years. Thanks, inflation.

Dollar Tree and spending habits

Shares of Dollar Tree rose 18% on May 28 after the retailer reported a 7.2% in quarterly net sales and a 19.7% increase in earnings per share.

It's a pretty safe bet that you have been shocked by certain price increases over the past few years. This can range from insurance to fuel to dining out, and can cause changes in behavior and spending patterns.

Tomi Kilgore covered Dollar Tree's (DLTR) latest financial results, which thrilled investors and provided insight into how inflation has affected spending habits.

Bill Peters explained how retailers such as Ross and Walmart were trying to score points with younger shoppers.

More coverage tied to personal spending:

-- Can jewelry, kids' clothes and 'KPop Demon Hunters' resurrect Kohl's from the dead?

-- Investors betting the Iran war is ending are buying up travel stocks

Is this a good way for you to pre-play the SpaceX IPO?

SpaceX filed for an initial public offering on May 20, but the IPO hasn't been priced yet.

But private and institutional investors have held shares of SpaceX for years. What if you could buy some of those shares indirectly before the IPO? Brett Arends dug into one well-known mutual fund that holds shares of SpaceX and shared what he learned about its opportunities and potential risk for investors.

Read on:

-- Space stocks tumble after a Blue Origin rocket explodes and SpaceX's valuation gets a reality check

-- Elon Musk's SpaceX IPO could quickly launch into your retirement plan

How not to talk about money

Quentin Fottrell is the Moneyist.

Quentin Fottrell - the Moneyist - answered questions from a reader who tries hard to hide her financial situation from her friends. This ties into the Moneyist's advice for people who are considering getting married.

More advice from the Moneyist:

-- I'm setting up brokerage accounts for my grandkids in my daughter's name. Is that wise - or am I asking for trouble?

-- 'Fairness is important to us': We sold our family business to our son at a discount. How can we make this up to our other child?

-- My friend, 62, earns $20,000 a year. Should she take Social Security now - and claim survivor's benefit at 67?

-- My golf buddy dropped me when I didn't ask him to be my financial adviser. Was I naive?

Want more from MarketWatch? Sign up for this and other newsletters to get the latest news and advice on personal finance and investing.

-Philip van Doorn

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-29-26 1423ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Better Hearing and Whole-Body Health: What to Know About MRI Access with Cochlear Implants
data.description:
Better Hearing and Whole-Body Health: What to Know About MRI Access with Cochlear Implants

Better Hearing and Whole-Body Health: What to Know About MRI Access with Cochlear Implants

PR Newswire

LONE TREE, Colo., May 29, 2026

During Better Hearing and Speech Month, understanding MRI compatibility is an important part of long-term hearing care

LONE TREE, Colo., May 29, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- May is Better Hearing and Speech Month, a month dedicated to raising awareness about the importance of hearing health. For the millions of people who experience hearing loss in the US, this month serves as a reminder that better hearing is not just about improved communication, it is a critical component of comprehensive health and wellbeing.

As the month comes to a close, Cochlear is encouraging those with hearing loss to think beyond hearing and think about how hearing solutions fit into overall lifelong health needs.

For example, one of the most common questions people ask before choosing a cochlear implant is: "Can you get an MRI with a cochlear implant?"

MRI access is an important part of long-term health planning

Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a widely used diagnostic tool. Over a lifetime, many people will need an MRI scan for reasons unrelated to hearing, such as orthopedic injuries, neurological conditions, or cancer screening.

For those considering a cochlear implant, understanding MRI access can be a critical part of informed decision-making.

Cochlear implants and MRI: what patients should know

Advances in cochlear implant technology have made MRI access easier and more convenient than ever before. For example, Cochlear's newest Nexa Series Implants (CI1000 Nexa) and the Profile Plus Series (CI600):

  • Provide access to MRI at both 1.5 Tesla and 3.0 Tesla with the magnet in place, which means no need for magnet removal¹

  • Do not require the MRI kit for these MRI scans¹

  • Patients do not need to wait to undergo an MRI or spend unnecessary time without sound¹

These features are designed to help patients maintain care without added procedures or delays, which can add peace of mind about choosing a cochlear implant.

Addressing common misconceptions

MRI compatibility is an important topic across all cochlear implant manufacturers, as all current-generation implants from all major companies have MRI conditional approval.1-4

What matters most when it comes to cochlear implants and MRI capability is not how many scans a device may claim to allow, but how easily and safely patients can get the imaging they need. Cochlear Nucleus implants (CI1000 Nexa and CI600) are designed to support routine access to MRI at commonly used strengths (1.5T and 3.0T) without magnet removal, helping patients avoid additional procedures or time without sound. This helps create an easily manageable experience, so patients and their care teams can focus on what matters most, getting the right care at the right time.

Hearing health is whole health

Better Hearing and Speech Month is a great reminder for individuals to ask questions, understand their options, and choose solutions that support both hearing and long-term health.

"Better hearing is about more than sound, it's about supporting a person's overall health throughout their life," Dr. Brian Kaplan MD, FACS, Cochlear Senior Vice President, Global Clinical Strategy & Innovation said.

"When choosing a cochlear implant, it's important to consider not only hearing performance, but also how that choice supports ongoing medical care, like access to MRI when it's needed."

When choosing Cochlear, patients gain access to a system designed with proven reliability, long-term MRI access, and patient-centered innovation.

About Cochlear Limited (ASX: COH)

People have always been Cochlear's inspiration, ever since Professor Graeme Clark set out to create the first multi-channel cochlear implant after seeing his father struggle with hearing loss. Since 1981, Cochlear has provided more than 750,000 devices in more than 180 countries, helping people of all ages around the world to hear. As the global leader in implantable hearing solutions, Cochlear connects people with life's opportunities, and welcomes them to the world's largest hearing implant community.

Cochlear has a global workforce of more than 5,500 people, with a passion for progress, who strive to meet the needs of people living with hearing loss. The company continually innovates to anticipate future needs, investing more than AUD$3 billion to date in research and development to push the boundaries of technology and help more people hear.

www.cochlear.com

References

  1. Cochlear Limited. D2044134. Cochlear™ Nucleus® Implants Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) Guidelines.
  2. https://www.medel.com/docs/default-source/isi-important-safety-information/languages/en-us-english-usa/ci-cochlear-implants/aw33290_90_manual-medical-procedures-ci-systems-en-english-us_web.pdf?auto=format&sfvrsn=b5299142_7 
  3. https://www.advancedbionics.com/content/dam/advancedbionics/documents/regional/us/professionals/hires-ultra-3d-mri-safety-information.pdf
  4. https://assets.cochlear.com/api/public/content/D2044134_2_2025-07-21?v=0e6e7eaa

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/better-hearing-and-whole-body-health-what-to-know-about-mri-access-with-cochlear-implants-302785815.html

SOURCE Cochlear North America

data.title: Oil prices tumble 20% in May - the biggest monthly drop since 2020. Here's what's next.
data.description:
By Isabel Wang and Myra P. Saefong

Angst on Wall Street about being caught off-guard if the U.S.-Iran war ends quickly sent global oil prices tumbling more than 20% in May. Both the U.S. and global benchmarks are now on pace for their biggest monthly swoon since the start of the onset of the COVID-19 crisis more than six years ago.

Brent crude saw its July contract (BRN00) (BRNN26) fall nearly 20% in May to $90.71 a barrel on Friday, the last trading day of the month and for the front-month contract. The international benchmark was on track for its largest one-month net and percentage decline since March 2020, when the pandemic triggered a historic demand shock that crashed global oil markets.

West Texas Intermediate crude for July contract (CL.1) (CLN26) has fallen 17% this month to $86.83 a barrel, also capping its worst month since early 2020 and snapping a four-month winning streak, according to FactSet data.

See: Oil prices slide as Trump says the U.S. is close to an Iran deal

Traders have been betting on an upcoming cease-fire deal that could ease supply disruptions in the Persian Gulf and resume global trade of the world's most important commodity. Negotiators from the U.S. and Iran have reportedly reached a tentative agreement to end the conflict in the Middle East, though any deal still needs approval from President Donald Trump and Iranian leaders.

In a post on Truth Social on Friday morning, Trump said he was meeting in the White House Situation Room "to make a final determination" on an agreement. It was unclear how long the meeting would last.

"The 60-day cease-fire may be a more noteworthy step towards a lasting end to the war, but the markets began to price in that 'track' in early-April when the initial, albeit more temporary in nature, ceasefire was declared," said Tyler Richey, technical analyst at Sevens Report.

The potential cease-fire has sparked some relief across the energy industry. Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said on Friday that the psychology of the market has been "this is closer to the end, rather than the beginning."

"There's this belief that the end is near, the conflict is nearly resolved, and the flow through the [Strait of Hormuz] will resume very quickly, and that has kept the backend of the curve lower than it might otherwise have been," Wirth said in an interview with Bloomberg.

Still, anxiety has been running high on Wall Street about the risks of getting the magnitude of this oil shock all wrong.

As part of this, commodity experts have been grappling with how much weight to give to the latest developments on the diplomatic front versus the looming potential for catastrophe and threat of $200-a-barrel oil if the conflict drags on much longer.

Helima Croft, head of global commodity strategy at RBC Capital, called it a "Memento mindset," with traders treating each "over-soon" comment from the Trump administration as "breaking news despite the ongoing diplomatic deadlock over Iran's nuclear program, control over the Strait, and repeated flare-ups of fighting has also kept a lid on prices."

Yet with the war now in its third month, "the world's energy shock absorbers are rapidly eroding, and time is running out to reopen the Strait and stave off a hard landing," Croft's team wrote in a Thursday client note.

See: The Iran war may be winding down, but the era of $60 oil could be over

Despite the roughly 20% monthly drop in May, both WTI and Brent remain meaningfully above prewar levels of around $60 a barrel, suggesting doubts that a cease-fire alone can fully normalize flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

"The headline roulette is reaching the point where it is becoming exhausting for oil traders," said Stephen Innes, managing partner at SPI Asset Management. "Every day seems to bring another cease-fire rumor, another missile headline, another draft memorandum, another denial, and another market reversal."

Under normal circumstances, oil prices could have risen "substantially," given the scale of the disruption, including the closure of the strait, constrained Iranian crude supply and declining global inventories, Innes said.

His view is that Brent could find a settling point around $90 a barrel, but that any return to normalcy might not happen soon, nor at all.

Markets have been simultaneously pricing in two opposing scenarios, he said. One assumes that crude flows gradually resume under "a negotiated framework," and another where disruptions deepen and supplies tighten further.

"Every headline effectively shifts probability between those two outcomes, which explains the violent day-to-day swings," he told MarketWatch on Friday.

Joy Wiltermuth and Claudia Assis contributed

-Isabel Wang -Myra P. Saefong

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-29-26 1402ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Neil Patel Joins HighLevel as Strategic Partner to Close the AI Gap Facing America's Small Businesses
data.description:
Neil Patel Joins HighLevel as Strategic Partner to Close the AI Gap Facing America's Small Businesses

Neil Patel Joins HighLevel as Strategic Partner to Close the AI Gap Facing America's Small Businesses

PR Newswire

DALLAS, May 29, 2026

Marketing's most followed voice bets on HighLevel as the AI platform built for the businesses left out of the Fortune 500 AI boom

DALLAS, May 29, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- HighLevel, one of the fastest growing AI platforms serving small and medium sized businesses, today announced a multi year strategic partnership with Neil Patel, founder of NP Digital and one of the most widely followed voices in marketing and SMB growth.

The partnership lands at an inflection point in the AI economy. Fortune 500 companies are spending billions deploying AI to cut costs and consolidate power, while the 36 million U.S. small businesses that employ nearly half of America's workforce are increasingly priced out, overwhelmed, and unable to compete. HighLevel is building the alternative. One platform with the tools businesses need to grow and scale, with AI embedded across websites, automations, CRM, Google Business Profiles, and more.

"For years, HighLevel has been called a fast growing SaaS company," said Neil Patel. "In 2026, it's time we start calling them what they are. One of the most important AI transformation platforms serving and scaling small and medium businesses today. The AI race shouldn't be a privilege of the Fortune 500. It should be the great equalizer."

Under the agreement, Patel and NP Digital will collaborate with HighLevel across product and AI roadmap, including co developed AI and SEO features; content and thought leadership, anchored by a new SMB focused podcast with HighLevel as founding sponsor; international expansion, drawing on NP Digital's UK and Australia footprint; and featured appearances at HighLevel's LevelUp event and the company's Summer of AI initiative in August 2026.

What makes this partnership powerful is that we share the same belief that AI should empower entrepreneurs, not confuse them. Together, we're focused on building technology that helps agencies and SMBs grow faster, compete smarter, and operate at a level that was previously only possible for large enterprises." Robin Alex, Co-founder, HighLevel

The dedicated partnership hub is live today at gohighlevel.com/neilpatel.

About HighLevel:
HighLevel is the AI-powered business operating system built for agencies, entrepreneurs, and operators who want intelligence embedded directly into how their business runs. The platform unifies CRM, messaging, automation, and revenue workflows into a single system where teams can work alongside AI to create, build, and strategize — or deploy AI agents that execute tasks autonomously. By building intelligence into the operational foundation rather than bolting it on as a feature, HighLevel helps businesses move faster, cut complexity, and scale more predictably in an AI-first economy.

About NP Digital
NP Digital is a global digital marketing agency powered by proprietary technology and a performance-driven approach, helping businesses increase visibility, drive revenue, and scale efficiently across channels. NP Digital takes a consultative, hands-on approach to marketing — combining strategy, data, and specialized execution to build long-term partnerships that deliver measurable growth.

Media Contacts

Piper McCoy

piper.mccoy@gohighlevel.com

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/neil-patel-joins-highlevel-as-strategic-partner-to-close-the-ai-gap-facing-americas-small-businesses-302785973.html

SOURCE HighLevel

data.title: Donaldson Company Increases Quarterly Cash Dividend 6.7%
data.description:
Donaldson Company Increases Quarterly Cash Dividend 6.7%

Donaldson Company, Inc. (NYSE: DCI) today announced that its Board of Directors declared a regular cash dividend of 32.0 cents per share, an increase of 6.7% from the prior quarterly dividend of 30.0 cents per share. The dividend is payable June 30, 2026, to shareholders of record on June 15, 2026. Donaldson is a member of the S&P High-Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index and calendar year 2025 marked the 30th consecutive year of annual dividend increases. The Company has paid a cash dividend every quarter for 70 years.

About Donaldson Company, Inc.

Founded in 1915, Donaldson (NYSE: DCI) is a global leader in technology-led filtration products and solutions, serving a broad range of industries and advanced markets. Diverse, skilled employees at over 140 locations on six continents partner with customers—from small business owners to R&D organizations and the world’s biggest OEM brands. Donaldson solves complex filtration challenges through three primary segments: Mobile Solutions, Industrial Solutions and Life Sciences. Additional information is available at www.Donaldson.com.

Sarika Dhadwal (952) 887-3753
Sarika.Dhadwal@Donaldson.com

data.title: Zehl & Associates Monitoring Deadly Oak Cliff Apartment Explosion in Dallas
data.description:
Zehl & Associates Monitoring Deadly Oak Cliff Apartment Explosion in Dallas

Incident believed to have been caused by a natural gas rupture

Dallas Fire-Rescue crews are investigating and searching debris in the wake of the May 28th natural gas explosion at a residential apartment building in Oak Cliff near Dallas' Bishop Arts District, and the experienced trial attorneys of respected Texas-based personal injury law firm Zehl & Associates are monitoring the situation closely.

The most recent reports list three dead, including a child, and at least four injured. The cause of the fire remains under investigation, but initial reports suggest a contractor working at the apartment complex may have struck a gas line.

"This is an especially tragic situation for everyone involved," said Ryan Zehl, the founder of Zehl & Associates. "The people caught in these kinds of disasters need all the assistance and resources we can provide, from understanding what their legal options are, to helping ensure evidence is preserved, finding them the best medical care available, and holding the parties at fault accountable."

While every investigation is unique, these disasters frequently involve preventable safety failures such as:

  • Improper utility-locating procedures
  • Failure to follow excavation safety protocols
  • Defective or poorly maintained gas infrastructure
  • Fire suppression system failures
  • Building code violations
  • Inadequate emergency shutoff procedures
  • Negligent property maintenance
  • Faulty smoke detectors or alarm systems

For more than two decades, Zehl & Associates has represented victims and families affected by some of the worst gas explosions and industrial fires in Texas history, including the Fort Worth Sandman Hotel Explosion in January 2024, a case where the firm is representing more than 20 employees, guests, and family members who were injured.

Survivors of gas explosions often suffer severe, life-altering injuries, including burns, traumatic brain injuries, lung damage from smoke or chemical inhalation, fractures and crush injuries from collapsing debris, amputations, emotional trauma, and PTSD. Many survivors face lasting physical, emotional, and financial consequences long after the headlines fade.

If you or someone you know has been affected by this disaster, we encourage you to contact Zehl & Associates at 1-888-486-8217.

About Zehl & Associates

Zehl & Associates is an undefeated personal injury law firm that represents accident victims across Texas, Louisiana and the United States, earning a reputation for recovering record-breaking verdicts and settlements for its clients. The firm’s practice areas include truck and 18-wheeler accidents, oilfield, maritime and refinery accidents, and catastrophic personal injury and wrongful death cases. The firm has secured billions of dollars for its clients, obtaining unprecedented victories against the largest trucking, oil and gas, maritime and petrochemical companies in the world. Learn more at https://www.zehllaw.com/.

Media Contact:
Jason Cunningham
jason@androvett.com
800-559-4534

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260529053401/en/

data.title: In HelloNation, HVAC Experts Dan and Matt Ambrose Explain HVAC Repair Cost in Rochester, NY, and What Affects Pricing
data.description:
In HelloNation, HVAC Experts Dan and Matt Ambrose Explain HVAC Repair Cost in Rochester, NY, and What Affects Pricing

In HelloNation, HVAC Experts Dan and Matt Ambrose Explain HVAC Repair Cost in Rochester, NY, and What Affects Pricing

PR Newswire

ROCHESTER, N.Y., May 29, 2026

The article outlines key cost factors that influence HVAC repairs and helps homeowners understand pricing expectations.

ROCHESTER, N.Y., May 29, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- What determines the cost of HVAC repair in Rochester, NY? The answer is explored in a HelloNation article that examines the key factors influencing HVAC repair cost in Rochester, NY, with insights from Dan and Matt Ambrose of Ambrose Mechanical Services Corp.

The HelloNation article explains that HVAC repair costs in Rochester, NY, vary widely depending on the type of issue and the system involved. Minor repairs, such as thermostat replacements or clearing clogged drain lines, typically involve lower HVAC labor costs and fewer parts. In contrast, complex issues such as compressor failure or electrical problems often require more time and specialized components, increasing the total expense.

The article describes furnace repair pricing as another major factor for homeowners in Rochester. Cold winters place heavy demand on heating systems, which can lead to wear and tear over time. Heating system repair for issues such as ignition failure, blower motor problems, or heat exchanger concerns often requires a detailed diagnosis. The article notes that older furnaces may further increase furnace repair pricing due to harder-to-find parts and additional labor time.

System type also plays an important role in HVAC repair factors. The article explains that central air systems, ductless mini-splits, and boiler systems each have unique repair requirements. Some systems allow easier access for technicians, while others require more extensive inspection, which directly affects HVAC labor costs. These differences contribute to the overall variation in HVAC repair costs in Rochester, NY.

Emergency HVAC repairs are highlighted as a consistent driver of higher costs. The article notes that urgent service requests at night, on weekends, or during holidays typically result in higher prices due to scheduling demands and the need for rapid response. Emergency HVAC repairs often require immediate troubleshooting and repair, which can increase labor and service fees compared to standard appointments.

Labor time is identified as one of the most consistent contributors to HVAC repair factors. The article explains that a repair requiring several hours or multiple visits will naturally cost more than a quick fix. Diagnostic time is also included in HVAC labor costs, particularly when the issue is complex or not immediately visible. This reinforces the importance of accurate assessments in determining final repair pricing.

Parts and materials further influence the overall expense of heating system repair. The article notes that commonly available components tend to be more affordable, while specialized or outdated parts can increase both cost and repair time. These material considerations are central to understanding HVAC repair factors and how they shape total project expenses.

The HelloNation article also emphasizes the value of HVAC maintenance in controlling long-term costs. Regular inspections and tune-ups can reduce the likelihood of unexpected breakdowns and help identify small issues before they become major problems. Consistent HVAC maintenance supports more predictable furnace repair pricing and reduces the need for frequent emergency HVAC repairs.

For homeowners in Rochester, NY, the article provides a clear overview of how HVAC repair costs are determined. By understanding HVAC repair factors such as system type, labor time, parts, and urgency, individuals can make informed decisions about repairs and ongoing HVAC maintenance.

How Much Does HVAC Repair Cost in Rochester, NY, and What Affects the Price features insights from Dan and Matt Ambrose, HVAC experts in Rochester, New York, on HelloNation.

About HelloNation

Through its community-focused digital publications and innovative "edvertising" approach, HelloNation delivers expert-driven, good-news content that informs, inspires, and spotlights the leaders making a meaningful impact in their communities. data.title: 'Fairness is important to us': We sold our family business to our son at a discount. How can we make this up to our other child?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'We did not seek or consider other offers. We engaged several professionals throughout the process.'

"We have sufficient liquidity to make gifts, if appropriate." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

We sold our successful, multigenerational family business to our son. Through the company, he is making payments over 15 years for both the business and the associated properties. He is 34, married, with no children and is earning a substantial salary, benefits, bonuses and other business-related perks. He is not experiencing personal financial strain, as we ensured the business could comfortably support the required payments.

A family discount was applied, but the purchase price was close to fair market value. We did not seek or consider other offers. We engaged several professionals throughout the process, and all parties are satisfied with the arrangement. What, if anything, should we do for our other child (who is married with one child and not involved in the business) to help equalize their inheritance? We have sufficient liquidity to make gifts, if appropriate.

We also filed IRS Form 709 in connection with the business transaction. Fairness is important to us.

The Parents

Don't miss: 'I plan to exit corporate life': I'm 50 and have $400,000. My wife is a teacher. Can I retire at 55?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Resentment and misunderstanding can grow in silence.

Dear Parents,

Keep those financial advisers on speed dial.

They did half their job, but there's more to do. Structuring this sale at the right price for your son, rather than another third party, and organizing payments in return over a 15-year period to reflect the value of the transaction is the first step in a two-step process. But it would be unfair to your other son to attempt to figure out the finances for his inheritance on your own. It appears that the sale price, when completed, is the transactional side, while the discount (IRS Form 709) represents the gift portion.

Call back your advisers and ask them to do the same thing they did for the sale of the family business, and that's structure an inheritance for your other son that takes into account the value of the discount. You are not trying to recreate the first deal in valuation terms. It does not have to be a dollar-for-dollar match because there are so many variables, not least the operational risk your son will carry ensuring that the success of the family business continues.

In practice, estate planning does not typically involve creating a counter-transaction. Instead, the business sale - along with any gift element - stands on its own. For example, you could make lifetime cash gifts to your other child, fund a trust for his benefit and/or adjust your wills so that the eventual distribution of your assets reflects the family-business sale. It's best to clearly communicate your plans in advance. If you have not done that, you can clearly outline your plans in a letter of intent.

Don't miss: 'He threatened to cut the roots': A tree fell onto my neighbor's property during a storm. Then all hell broke loose.

Talk about your plans

Sit down with your other son and talk to him about your plans, in broad terms. No one needs to know your net worth, but it would not hurt to reassure him that you are putting in place a mechanism that ensures they will both be treated fairly and, to the best of your ability, equally. He may have suggestions about receiving a portion of his inheritance early (if, say, he wants to purchase a house). You can take them under advisement without fear or prejudice.

Ultimately, resentment and misunderstanding can grow in silence. So the more dialogue you have with your sons, your financial advisers and your spouse, the better. Usually, this column will check your numbers and give you options about investing and saving for retirement, on occasion even nudging you into taking no action, but this dilemma deserves to be handed over to a trust and estate attorney and/or adviser who can write you a detailed estate plan, one that takes your other son's interests into account.

One Moneyist reader has been through something eerily similar. "In this scenario, I am the 'other' sibling, and although it was purely accidental, that didn't make it hurt any less," she wrote. "I've seen how situations like this can destroy families, and I've had to overlook a lot to avoid feeling a certain way. In my case, the family business was gifted to my sibling, who had both the interest and the ability to run it, rather than being sold. "My parents' reasoning was, 'Your sibling wanted it; you did not.'"

Another Moneyist reader recognizes your willingness to do the right thing. "I don't have an answer," she wrote, "but I want to share what I've witnessed in family dynamics after the passing of parents. I know two families who are now on the other side of a similar situation. In both cases, there are siblings - now in their 60s and 70s - who no longer speak to each other due to the unclear way their parents left things and the subsequent vitriol after their passing. You need to resolve this now - and honestly."

And that's exactly what you intend to do.

Related: 'I'm an endless honey pot': My wife and I are in our 70s. We gave our son $40,000 for his L.A. wedding. Now he wants more.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'It feels awkward': I gave my friend's daughter cash for her wedding. Silence. Do I say something?

'It is such a fine line': I am a CFP and see nothing wrong with networking on the golf course. Am I wrong?

'I worked very hard': I'm 71 and have $6 million after scrimping and saving. My son, 33, wants money for a house. Do I say yes?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-29-26 1330ET
